segments.s402-g1: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when' (introducing a time clause). The phrase follows a typical Persian order: subject 'ماه' (the moon) before the past tense verb 'نشست' (sank/settled). 'از بر تخت سیمین' is a prepositional phrase 'from (از) upon (بر) the silver throne' — the double preposition is an archaic/poetic stacking meaning 'from off the silver throne'.
segments.s402-g2: This is a time-elapsed clause: 'سه پاس ... گذشت' = 'three passes/watches passed'. 'سه پاس' is a quantitative phrase (three watches), 'از شب تیره' specifies 'of the dark night' with the genitival sense of از, and 'اندر گذشت' ('passed within') uses poetic 'اندر' for 'in/within' plus the past 'گذشت' meaning 'passed/elapsed'.
segments.s402-g3: همی is an archaic/poetic particle giving immediacy or emphasis (here rendered 'then'/'just so'). 'پاسبان' is the subject 'the guard', and 'برخروشید' is a past tense verb meaning 'rose up/roared' with 'سخت' modifying it adverbially ('severely/strongly' = 'loudly'). Word order is subject + verb + adverbial intensifier.
segments.s402-g4: که introduces reported content here ('that') rather than a causal link. The clause states 'گشتاسپ شاهست' (Gushtasp is king) with the archaic copula-form 'شاهست' ('is king'), then 'و پیروز بخت' coordinates with 'و' to say 'and (is) victorious by fate' — 'بخت' = fortune/fate, linked adjectivally to 'پیروز'.
segments.s402-g5: چو is the temporal 'when'. 'ترکان' is the subject 'the Turks', and 'شنیدند' is the past tense verb 'they heard'. 'زان سان خروش' is a noun phrase 'that such cry' ('زان' = 'from/that', 'سان' = 'such/so', 'خروش' = 'cry/roar'), indicating what was heard.
segments.s402-g6: نهادند is the past tense of نهادن 'they put/placed' used here idiomatically with 'یکسر به آواز گوش' — 'they at once put (their attention) to the voice', i.e. 'they all listened at once'. یکسر is an adverb 'entirely/at once', and 'به آواز گوش' is a dative phrase 'to the voice/ear' meaning 'to the sound' or 'gave ear'.
segments.s402-g7: 'دل کهرم' reads as a possessive/construct 'the heart of Kahram' (Kahram is a proper name here). 'از پاسبان' is an ablative 'by/from the guard' indicating cause, and 'خیره شد' is a compound of the adjective 'خیره' ('astonished/stunned') with the verb 'شد' 'became' — 'his heart became stunned'.
segments.s402-g8: 'روانش' = 'his soul/spirit' (روان + possessive suffix), 'ز آواز او' = 'because of/from his voice' (ز = from/because of, او = he/his), and 'تیره شد' = 'became dark' (تیره = dark/bleak; شد = became). Together this states his spirit was darkened by that voice.
segments.s402-g105: هرانکس که شد در دم اژدها is a relative clause introduced by the fused phrase هرانکس که (= هر آن کس که, 'whoever who'). شد is the past tense of شدن used here to mean 'found himself / came to be' in a situation. در دم اژدها is an adverbial phrase ('in/at the dragon's breath or instant') placed after the verb to specify the circumstance.
segments.s402-g106: بکوشید is a past-tense, third-person singular/plural form meaning 'he/they strove/struggled'. و هم introduces a contrast ('and yet/even so'). ز + (و) is the poetic preposition 'from/of' (زو = از او), and نیامد رها is an inverted verbal phrase: نیامد 'did not come/happen' with رها functioning as a predicative adjective 'free/unbound' — together meaning 'could not be freed (from it)'.
segments.s402-g107: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from/of' marking the source group. ترکان چینی lists the two peoples (Turks and Chinese) as the topic; فراوان is an adverb/adjective 'many' modifying the following verb; نماند is a past negative meaning 'did not remain / were not left' (final verb completes the clause). Word order places the source and nouns before the verb, typical of Persian narrative.
segments.s402-g108: وگر is the conditional 'but if / and if'. ماند is the past-tense 'remained'. The clause کس نام ایشان نخواند is the consequence: کس here functions negatively ('no one'), نام ایشان 'their name(s)', and نخواند is the negative past/hypothetical form of خواندن used here in the sense 'would not call/utter' — together meaning 'if any remained, no one would call their names.'
segments.s402-g109: همه is the plural subject 'all/everyone'. ترگ و جوشن is a coordinated noun phrase 'rags and cuirass/armor'. فرو ریختند is a compound verbal expression: فرو (downward) + ریختند (past of ریختن 'to fall/pour'), meaning 'fell down / were torn off'. The plural verb agrees with the plural subject.
segments.s402-g110: هم از indicates continuation 'also/from' linking to the previous clause. دیده‌ها is the plural noun 'the eyes' (agent/source), and خون برآمیختند places the verb last: خون (blood) + برآمیختند (they mingled/was mixed) — meaning blood mingled/flowed (with tears). The prefix بر- gives the sense of 'together / upon', so the clause paints blood mixing from the eyes.
segments.s402-g111: دوان is a verbal form meaning 'they ran / rushed' (manner of motion). پیش اسفندیار is a prepositional phrase 'before / in the presence of Esfandiyar' that marks the goal/recipient of the action. آمدند is the past plural 'they came' — the pair دوان ... آمدند is a rhetorical doubling of motion (they ran and came up to him).
segments.s402-g112: همه دیده is the subject 'all eyes'. چون is the simile marker 'like/as', جویبار is the comparison 'a stream', and آمدند (past plural) 'came' describes the motion/flow — poetically saying the eyes (tears) came/flowed like a stream. Word order keeps the simile between subject and verb.
segments.s402-g129: بفرمود is an honorific past tense verb meaning 'he ordered/said' and introduces a subordinate clause with تا used here as a subordinator 'that/so that'. آتش (noun) + اندر (archaic locative preposition 'in/within') precede زدند, a 3rd-person plural past verb (زدن) which with آتش forms the idiom 'آتش زدن' = 'to set fire'. The overall structure is: [he-ordered] + تا + [fire in set-they] → 'he ordered that they set fire inside.'
segments.s402-g130: همه شهر توران is a noun phrase meaning 'the entire city of Turan' (همه premodifier + شهر head + توران proper name). بهم بر زدند is an older/poetic verbal phrase: بهم is adverbial ('together, into confusion'), بر is a prefix/preposition involved in the compound, and زدند is the 3rd-person plural past verb. The clause functions as a result: 'the whole city of Turan fell into disorder/was thrown into confusion.'
segments.s402-g131: به جایی دگر is a locative/contrastive phrase 'in another place / anywhere else' (به + جایی + دگر). نامداری is a nominal subject 'fame/renown'. نماند is the past negative form of ماندن ('did not remain/was not left'). Word order places the locative fronted for emphasis: '[In other places] fame did not remain.'
segments.s402-g132: This repeats the negative-stative structure with locative phrases: به چین و به توران are two prepositional locatives 'in China and in Turan'. سواری is the noun 'rider/horseman' (here collective, 'cavalry' or 'horsemen'). نماند again is 'remained not' (no longer existed). The clause means 'in China and in Turan no horsemen remained.'
segments.s402-g133: تو گفتی که is a reporting clause 'you said that'. ابری is a noun here 'a cloud' (postposed adjective follows), برآمد is the 3rd-person singular past 'arose/appeared', and سیاه is a postpositive adjective 'black/dark'. The clause therefore reads 'you said that a black cloud arose.'
segments.s402-g134: ببارید is a past tense verb meaning 'it rained/fell' (here used figuratively). آتش is the grammatical subject/agent 'fire' and thus 'fire rained' (i.e., flames or burning missiles fell). بران is a demonstrative/adverbial 'upon that' (thereon), and رزمگاه is the locative noun 'battlefield'. Together: 'fire rained upon that battlefield.'
segments.s402-g135: جهانجوی is an epithet/noun 'the world-seeker' (an ambitious/renowned man). چون ... دید here forms a temporal clause 'when he saw': چون (subordinator) + کار 'the affair/matter' + زان گونه 'in that manner' + دید (3rd-person past 'saw'). The phrase means 'when the world-seeker saw the affair in that way...'.
segments.s402-g136: سران را is a marked direct object ('the leaders' + object marker را). بیاورد is a past-tense (subjunctive/relative form used as narrative) meaning 'he brought' or 'he caused to come'. و links the two verbs, and می‌درکشید is a habitual/progressive imperfect formed with می‑ indicating ongoing or repeated action; it shares the subject with بیاورد. In context it reads 'he brought the leaders and was drawing (them) in / was pulling them (toward himself)'; the second verb is an archaic/poetic form.
segments.s402-g17: چه at the start is an exclamatory/interrogative 'what/how' asking rhetorically. بازی کند is a verb phrase where بازی (literally 'play') is used with کند ('does/makes') to mean 'what can (one) do / what trick/play will (one) attempt'; the finite verb کند appears before the subject (پاسبان) in this elevated/poetic word order. پاسبان is the logical subject 'watchman/guard', and روز جنگ is a temporal adverbial 'on the day of battle' modifying the whole action.
segments.s402-g18: برین is the prepositional phrase 'بر + این' meaning 'against/on these' and takes نامداران ('the famed/renowned ones') as its object. شود is the copular/impersonal 'become' (here in classical present sense). کار تنگ is a small predicative phrase: کار 'affair/matter' + تنگ 'narrow/difficult', together meaning 'the matter becomes tight / they are hard-pressed'. The clause states the result or situation with the famed men.
segments.s402-g19: وگر is the conditional 'if' (poetic/archaic variant of اگر). دشمن ما is the noun phrase 'our enemy' (demonstative possessor follows noun). بود is the copula in the conditional clause ('were' / 'would be'). خانگی is an adjective 'domestic, of the household' describing that enemy — i.e. 'if the enemy were domestic / one of our household.'
segments.s402-g20: بجوی is an older/poetic present form of جستن 'to seek' (here third-person meaning 'he will seek' or 'he seeks'); همی is the continuative particle meaning 'continually/always' that emphasises ongoing action. روز بیگانگی is a noun phrase 'a day/time of estrangement/alienation' (بیگانگی 'estrangement, foreignness'), the object or goal of the seeking. Together: 'he will continually seek a day of estrangement.'
segments.s402-g21: به introduces a manner/instrument phrase 'by/with'. آواز بد گفتن is an infinitival/verbal-noun construction meaning 'to utter an ill/ominous cry' (آواز 'voice/call', بد 'bad', گفتن 'to say/utter'). The coordination و فال بد parallels that action: 'and (to give) bad omen' — فال بد is 'an ill omen' or 'bad augury'. The whole phrase states the manner by which something is done.
segments.s402-g22: بکوبیم is an optative/subjunctive/poetic present first-person-plural of کوبیدن 'to strike/beat' and here expresses 'we will strike / let us strike'. مغزش is the object 'his brain' (مغز + possessive -ش). به marks the instrument or manner 'with', and گوپال (literally 'cowherd' or a proper name) appears with بد 'evil/ill' as its qualifier: 'with the evil cowherd' or 'with evil Gupal' — together conveying the violent instrument or insulting force used to batter him.
segments.s402-g23: بدین گونه is a fixed demonstrative phrase 'in this way / thus' (بدین = 'in this', گونه = 'manner'). آواز is the subject 'the cry/voice', and پیوسته شد is a compound predicate 'became continuous' (پیوسته 'continuous/constant' + شد 'became'). The clause reports that the shouting continued without pause.
segments.s402-g24: دل is the subject 'heart'; کهرم (an adjectival/epithetic form meaning 'my old/aged') qualifies the heart — together 'my aged heart'. از + پاسبان marks the cause or source ('from/because of the watchman'), خسته is the predicate adjective 'tired/weary', and شد is the copular verb 'became'. The clause says the speaker's old heart grew weary of the guard.
segments.s402-g113: سپهدار is the head noun meaning ‘commander’ followed by two descriptive predicates خونریز و بیداد joined with و; the copula بود (past, 3rd sg.) comes at the end, so the whole clause reads ‘the commander was bloody and oppressive.’ بیداد here functions as an adjectival/nominal quality ‘oppression’ applied to the commander.
segments.s402-g114: سپاهش is سپاه + enclitic ـش ‘his army’; the preposition به introduces the object of the emotion (broadly ‘with/regarding’), بیدادگر is a noun ‘oppressor/tyrant’ rather than an adjective here, and شاد بود is the predicate ‘was glad’ (شاد = happy; بود = past copula). Word order is possessive noun + prepositional phrase + predicate.
segments.s402-g115: کسی را is the definite/contrastive object phrase (‘to no one’ here because of the negative verb), placed before the verb; نداد is the negated past of دادن ‘did not give’. از یلان specifies the class (‘of the heroes’ / ‘among the heroes’). زینهار is the noun ‘warning’/‘admonition’ that is the thing not given.
segments.s402-g116: بکشتند is past plural ‘they killed/slaughtered’ with the archaic prefix بـ- retained in poetry. زان is a demonstrative/pronominal linker ‘from those’/‘of those’ referring back to the previously mentioned people; خستگان بی‌شمار is a noun phrase ‘countless weary ones’ that functions as the object of بکشتند.
segments.s402-g117: به توران زمین is a locative phrase ‘in (the) land of Turan’ (به + place + زمین), followed by شهریاری ‘sovereignty/rule’ as the subject and نماند the past negative/negated result ‘did not remain/was no more’. نماند is past tense meaning ‘remained’ (here negated in sense).
segments.s402-g118: ز ترکان چین is a poetic range/expanse ‘from the Turks to China’ (ز = from/of; چین = China used as a geographical extreme). نامداری ‘renown/fame’ is the thing that نماند ‘did not remain’; the clause asserts that fame was gone across that whole region.
segments.s402-g119: سراپرده و خیمه are two coordinated nouns ‘inner curtain/inner chamber and tent(s)’ joined by و; برداشتند is the past plural verb ‘they removed/took down’. Word order places the coordinated objects before the verb, as is common in Persian narrative.
segments.s402-g120: بدان is the fused prepositional phrase (به + آن) meaning ‘to those/for those’ (here ‘for those weary ones’); خستگان ‘the weary’ is the beneficiary/recipient, جای ‘a place’ is the object, and بگذاشتند (they left/they placed) is the past plural verb — here meaning they left/allocated a place for them. Note that in the word list دان’s provided senses do not match the demonstrative آن; functionally the unit بدآن = به + آن = ‘to those’.
segments.s402-g49: دو لشکر is the nominal subject 'two armies' (numeral + noun). بران سان is an adverbial phrase: بران (thereupon/onto that) + سان (like/so) giving a manner/ direction ('thereupon, in that manner'). برآشوفتند is a past-tense, 3rd-person-plural verb (compound with the prefix بر- + آشفتن) meaning 'they rushed upon' or 'they surged'. The phrase order (subject — adverbial phrase — verb) is typical of poetic Persian.
segments.s402-g50: همی is a verbal particle indicating continuous or iterative action here ('kept on' / 'continually'). بر سر یکدگر is a prepositional phrase 'upon the heads of one another' (بر = 'upon', سر = 'head', یکدگر = 'one another' / reciprocal). کوفتند is the past 3rd-person-plural of کوبیدن 'to strike/beat'. Word order places the iterative particle before the prepositional object and the verb at the end, yielding 'they kept striking one another on the head.'
segments.s402-g51: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' that frames the clause. تا here is the subordinating/temporal 'until'. برآمد is past tense (3rd-sg) 'arose' or 'came up'. سپیده‌دمان is the noun 'dawn/daybreak' used adverbially ('at dawn' / 'dawns'). Together the phrase means 'thus, until dawn rose.'
segments.s402-g52: بزرگان چین را is a nominal phrase 'the great men of Chin' (بزرگان = 'great ones', چین = 'Chin/China') with را marking definiteness/definite object or topical emphasis. سرآمد زمان is an epithet/noun phrase 'foremost (سرآمد) of the time/age (زمان)', placed after the noun as an appositive or epithet. In this poetic, verbless line the second phrase qualifies or apposes the first: 'the great men of Chin, the foremost of the age.'
segments.s402-g53: برفتند is the past-tense, 3rd-person-plural verb 'they went/advanced'. مردان اسفندیار is the subject 'the men of Esfandiyar' (noun + genitive/name). Note the verb precedes the subject here — an allowed poetic inversion (verb–subject order).
segments.s402-g54: بران is an adverbial 'thereupon/against that' indicating direction or reference back to the preceding action. نامور is an adjective 'renowned' modifying an understood target. باره here functions as a substantive/relational word ('in the matter/occasion of') that links the following noun شهریار ('the king') to the action; together باره شهریار conveys 'with respect to the king' or 'in the king's cause.' The whole phrase therefore reads as an adverbial/accusative cluster indicating they moved 'against that renowned one in the king's affair.'
segments.s402-g55: بریده is a participial/adjectival form 'cut off' or 'severed' placed before the noun (a common poetic order). سر 'head' is the noun being qualified; شاه ارجاسپ is the title + proper name 'King Arjasp'. را is the definite object marker attached to this noun phrase. The phrase is a nominal object (often standing in for the action's result) describing a severed head.
segments.s402-g56: جهاندار and خونیز لهراسپ را form a second coordinated object. جهاندار here is used as a title/epithet ('sovereign' / 'world-holder') or a proper epithet-name in apposition; و is the conjunction 'and'. خونیز is an adjective 'blood-stained' modifying لهراسپ (Lohrasp). The final را marks the whole coordinated phrase as a definite object as well. Poetic listing places these decapitated figures in a row: 'the severed head of King Arjasp, and Jahandar, and blood-stained Lohrasp.'
segments.s402-g89: This is a simple SOV clause: the proper name/epithet تهمتن is the subject, followed by the object کمربند کهرم (belt of/associated with کهرم) with no ezafe (a common poetic omission), and then the past-tense verb گرفت (3rd‑singular). گرفت here means “seized/took hold of.”
segments.s402-g90: مر او را is the object phrase (او را = “him” with the definite object marker را); مر is an archaic/narrative particle/clitic that appears before the object here (used in epic diction). ازان پشت زین is a prepositional phrase ‘from that back of the saddle’ (ازان = ‘from that’ + پشت = ‘back’ + زین = ‘saddle’). برگرفت is a past verb (3rd‑singular) meaning ‘lifted up / took up,’ linking back to the object phrase.
segments.s402-g91: برآوردش is the verb برآورد with the enclitic object pronoun -ش = ‘him,’ here meaning ‘lifted/took up him.’ از جای = ‘from [his] place’ (از + جای). The conjunction و links to زد بر زمین, where زد (past tense ‘struck’) plus the prepositional phrase بر زمین (‘onto the ground’) expresses ‘struck him to the ground.’
segments.s402-g92: همه لشکرش is the subject phrase ‘his whole army/host’ (همه = all; لشکرش = his army). خواندند is the past plural verb ‘they cried/out,’ used here to mean ‘they shouted/called out.’ آفرین is the interjection shouted (‘Bravo!’). Word order places the shouted word after the verb as reported speech.
segments.s402-g93: دو دستش = ‘his two hands’ (دو + دست + possessive suffix ش). ببستند is a past plural verb ‘they tied/bound’ (here: they bound his hands). و connects to بردند (they took/carried him away). خوار is a predicative/adverbial describing the manner — ‘despised / humiliated / lowly’ — implying they carried him off in disgrace.
segments.s402-g94: پراگنده شد is a light-verb construction meaning ‘became scattered’ (پراگنده = scattered; شد = became). Note the postverbal subject لشکر نامدار (‘the renowned host’) — inversion is common in poetic narrative, so ‘the renowned army was scattered’ is the natural reading.
segments.s402-g95: همی is a frequent epic particle indicating continuous/iterative action (‘kept’ / ‘was’); گرز is the subject ‘maces’ and بارید is the past verb ‘fell’/‘rained.’ همچون تگرگ is a simile ‘like hail.’ Together: ‘maces rained down like hail.’
segments.s402-g96: This is a parallel clause: زمین پر ز ترگ = ‘the ground full of shreds/rags’ (پر ز = ‘full of’ + ترگ = ‘shreds’), و هوا پر ز مرگ = ‘and the air full of death.’ The repetition of پر ز creates a balanced, emphatic poetic image.
segments.s402-g57: به پیش سپاه اندر انداختند is a prepositional phrase + verb in the simple past (3rd plural). به پیش سپاه (literally 'to/in front of the host') places the action spatially; اندر is a classical preposition 'in/into' that combines with انداختند (past 3pl of انداختن) meaning 'they threw/cast'. The direct object is omitted (typical in epic listing; whatever was brought earlier is implied). Word order here places the locative phrase before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s402-g58: ز پیکار ترکان بپرداختند begins with the poetic preposition ز 'from/because of' followed by the noun phrase پیکار ترکان ('the battle of the Turks' — پیکار head, ترکان genitive). بپرداختند is past (3pl) of پرداختن with the prefixed ب-; in classical usage it means 'turned to' / 'set about' (here: engaged/attacked) rather than the literal 'paid'. The clause indicates they directed their action toward the Turks' fight.
segments.s402-g59: خروشی برآمد ز توران سپاه is a simple past-clause: خروشی (a roar/clamour) is the subject noun, برآمد is the past tense 'arose/issued forth', and ز + توران سپاه ('from the Turanian host') marks source. Word order here is noun + verb + source, a normal narrative pattern in Farsi poetry.
segments.s402-g60: ز سر برگرفتند گردان کلاه literally 'from (their) heads they lifted (the) troop-helmets'. ز سر is a prepositional phrase 'from the head(s)'; برگرفتند is past (3pl) of برگرفتن (to take up / lift off). گردان کلاه is a noun phrase ('helmets' with a qualifying word); the whole clause means they removed or raised their helmets off their heads (a brisk, idiomatic martial action). The adjective/order is poetic and compact: object phrase follows the verb in this line.
segments.s402-g61: دو فرزند ارجاسپ گریان شدند: دو (numeral) precedes the noun فرزند (child/descendant); ارجاسپ follows as the possessive/genitive name 'of Arjasp'. گریان is a participial/adjectival form 'weeping', and شدند is past (3pl) of شدن used here as a copula/auxiliary — 'became weeping' = 'began to weep' or 'were weeping'. This is a standard nominal-predicative construction.
segments.s402-g62: چو بر آتش تیز بریان شدند: چو is the classical conjunction 'when'; بر آتش is the locative 'on/upon the fire'; تیز is an adjective 'sharp/fierce' following the noun (poetic adjective order); بریان is a participle/adjective 'roasted/seared', and شدند is the past copula — together indicating that, when they were seared by the keen flames, they cried. The clause uses participial + شدن to express a resulting state.
segments.s402-g63: بدانست لشکر که آن جنگ چیست is an instance of classical word order where the verb بدانست ('knew/realized') appears before the subject لشکر ('the army'). The clause که آن جنگ چیست is a subordinate nominal clause ('what that battle is/was') functioning as the object of بدانست; چی (what) + ست (is) form an embedded interrogative 'what kind/what is'.
segments.s402-g64: وزان رزم بد بر که باید گریست begins with وزان (a contraction of و ز آن, 'and/from that'), followed by رزم بد ('a bad/dreadful battle'). بر here is a connective/prepositional particle used in poetic register to link the consequence clause; که باید گریست is a result clause: که introduces 'that...' and باید is the impersonal/modal 'it is necessary/one must', followed by گریست (to weep/weep) — so the line states that because of that terrible combat it is fitting/necessary to lament.
segments.s402-g97: This is a noun phrase + simile: "سر از تیغ پران" literally places the noun 'سر' (head) before the source prepositional phrase 'از تیغ' (from the sword). The word 'پران' is an archaic/poetic verbal/adjectival element meaning 'sent flying/scattered' modifying 'سر'. "چو" introduces the simile 'like', followed by "برگ از درخت" (leaves from a tree) which parallels the earlier construction.
segments.s402-g98: Two short coordinated clauses joined by a correlative 'یکی ... و یکی ...' meaning 'one ... and another ...'. "ریخت" is used as a past action meaning 'poured/was shed' with the direct object "خون" (blood). In the second clause "یافت" is the past of 'to find/obtain' and takes "تخت" (throne) as its object; subjects are the indefinite 'یکی' in each clause.
segments.s402-g99: "همی" is a continuous/iterative marker meaning 'kept on' or 'still', so "همی موج زد" = 'waves kept striking' and is used metaphorically: "موج زد خون" means 'blood surged like a wave'. "بران" is a demonstrative/adverbial 'thereon'/'on that', and "رزمگاه" is the locative 'battlefield' governed by that adverbial.
segments.s402-g100: This is an elliptical, parallel noun phrase listing two contrasting states: "سری زیر نعل" = 'a head under a horseshoe' ("سری" = head; "زیر" precedes the noun it governs) and "سری با کلاه" = 'a head with a helmet' ("با" = 'with'). The repeated "سری" establishes the parallelism; the constructions are prepositional modifiers describing the state of the heads.
segments.s402-g101: "نداند" is the negative form of 'to know' (3rd person) and here precedes the noun "کسی": in negative contexts "کسی" functions as 'no one'. Word order is verb–subject–object (archaic/poetic). "آرزوی جهان" is a genitive noun phrase 'the world's desire' acting as the object of "نداند".
segments.s402-g102: "نخواهد" here functions as a future/negative auxiliary ('will not'), followed by the infinitive "گشادن" 'to open/reveal'. "بمابر" is a dative/adverbial meaning 'for us' or 'to us', and "نهان" is the object 'the hidden/secret'. Word order (auxiliary + infinitive + indirect object + object) is typical of classical/poetic Persian.
segments.s402-g103: "کسی کش" is a compound: "کسی" (someone/anyone) plus the agentive element "کش" (maker/doer) yields 'one who kills someone' or 'killer'. "سزاوار" is an adjective 'deserving' that takes the following noun phrase "بد بارگی" ('bad baragi') as the thing deserved; "بد بارگی" is an idiomatic/poetic phrase literally 'bad-leafed' or 'bad fate' used metaphorically here.
segments.s402-g104: "گریزان" is a participial/adjectival noun 'the fleeing one' used as subject. "همی راند" again uses "همی" for continuous aspect ('keeps on driving/urging'), with "راند" (past/preterite sense 'drove/expelled'); "یکبارگی" is an adverb 'suddenly/at once' that modifies the verbal action. Together they describe a sudden act associated with a fleeing person.
segments.s402-g121: بران here is the fused locative/particle (بر + آن) meaning 'on that / thereupon'. روی دژ is a noun phrase 'the face/surface of the fortress' (روی = surface/side + دژ = fortress). بر is the preposition 'upon/on', ستاره is the direct object 'a star' (poetic image), and بزد is the past simple 'he struck/smote'. Word order is subject omitted (typical Persian epic style) + verb at the end; the phrase as a whole means 'he struck the star upon the face of the fortress.'
segments.s402-g122: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. پیداشد is a light-verb construction (پیدا + شد) meaning 'became visible / appeared' (پیدا = visible; شد = became). از هر دری literally 'from every دری' ('از' = from; هر = every), and نیک و بد is the correlative pair 'good and bad'. Note: دری here functions as 'door / gate' in context (an older/poetic form); the clause reads 'when it became visible from every gate, good and bad.'
segments.s402-g123: بزد is the past verb 'he struck/struck at'. بر در دژ = 'against the gate of the fortress' (بر = against/on, در = gate/door, دژ = fortress). دو دار بلند is the object 'two tall دار' — here دار is best read as 'posts/trees/poles' (tall beams). Word order places the verb first in the segment but overall the clause is verb + prepositional phrase + object.
segments.s402-g124: فرو هشت is an older/poetic phrasing meaning 'he let down / he lowered' (فرو = downwards; هشت here is an archaic verbal element). از دار پیــچان کمند = 'from the (said) poles / beams the twisting/noose(s)'; از = from, دار = poles, پیچان = 'twisting/curled' (adjective), کمند = 'noose/lasso'. The phrase describes lowering twisted ropes from the poles.
segments.s402-g125: سر اندریمان is 'the head of Andarimān' (سر = head; اندریمان is a personal name). نگونسار is an adjective 'woeful / mournful'. کرد is the past verb 'made / did'. The construction 'X نگونسار کرد' is a causative/causal poetic turn: 'he made X mournful' — idiomatically here implying grievous harm to Andarimān (often 'beheaded' or greatly afflicted).
segments.s402-g126: برادرش = 'his brother' (برادر + enclitic ش = his). را is the definite object marker. نیز = 'also'. بر دار کرد is an idiomatic verbal phrase: بر دار ('on the gallows' / 'upon the gallows') + کرد ('he put / he did') = 'he hanged (him)'. Word order: object (with را) before the verbal phrase, typical Persian.
segments.s402-g127: سپاهی برون کرد = 'he sent out troops' (سپاهی = soldier/troop; برون = out/forth; کرد = did/sent). بر هر سوی = 'to every direction' (بر = to/toward, هر = every, سوی = side/direction). Structure: object-subject ellipsis is common; overall meaning is 'he dispatched soldiers in all directions.'
segments.s402-g128: به جایی که آمد = 'to the place(s) where they came' (به = to; جایی = place; که آمد = 'that/which came' used temporally/spatially). نشان گوی is a verbal phrase in which نشان = 'sign / signal' and گوی (here from گفتن) functions as 'to say/announce' (poetic/elliptical usage). Together: 'to announce the sign at the places they reached.'
segments.s402-g65: بگفتند is a past plural reporting verb 'they said/they cried out' used to introduce direct speech. The following words (رادا دلیرا سرا) are a string of vocatives/names following the verb; Persian allows naming or calling people directly after the speech verb without a separate particle.
segments.s402-g66: This segment names a military title plus an epithet and a personal name. سپهدار is the title 'commander' (used here as noun/title), شیراوژنا is an epithet meaning 'lion‑like' modifying the name, and مهترا is the proper name (the person being addressed or named). The three words stand in apposition: 'the commander, (the) lion‑like Mahtara.'
segments.s402-g67: The first که is the relative 'who/that' introducing 'the one who killed you' (کشتت = 'he killed you', past with 2nd‑person object suffix). The second که again links the relative clause to the optative predicate that follows. بر دشت is a prepositional phrase 'on the plain', کین is 'vengeance' and کشته + باد is a passive participial construction with باد as optative particle: 'may he be slain.'
segments.s402-g68: برو here is an imperative 'go!' addressed to someone (a messenger or runner). The following sequence is an optative clause with باد at the end: 'جاودان روز برگشته باد' = 'may the returned/day of return be eternal' (word order inverted for poetic effect: adjective + noun + participle + optative). باد marks the whole clause as a wish ('may ...').
segments.s402-g69: سپردن is the infinitive 'to entrust/hand over'; in this construction it functions as the kernel of a question or deliberation ('whom to entrust'). کرا is the archaic interrogative 'who/whom', باید is the modal 'must/should', اکنون is 'now', and بنه is an imperative 'put/place' (poetic/archaic imperative). The clause therefore asks whom to entrust and then issues the command to place something now.
segments.s402-g70: درفش is 'banner/standard' (the object to be placed). که is the relative pronoun 'that/which' introducing the relative clause 'which we have', داریم is present 'we have/possess', بر میمنه is a locative phrase 'on the right (side)' — together: 'the banner which we have on the right.'
segments.s402-g71: چو introduces a temporal clause ('when'). ارجاسپ is the proper name 'Arjasp'. پردخته شد is a passive or resultative sequence meaning 'was prepared/arrayed/put in order' (پردخته = composed/arrayed; شد = became/was), and قلبگاه is a noun 'heart/center/core' linked into the clause as the affected noun. The clause thus sets the temporal scene: 'When Arjasp was arrayed/prepared (regarding his heart/core)...'.
segments.s402-g72: مبادا is a fixed negative optative meaning 'may it not be / lest'; used twice here for parallel emphasis. کلاه in poetic/figurative contexts can mean 'trick/deception' (rather than literal 'hat'), و is the coordinating 'and', and سپاه is 'army/host'. Grammatically this is a pair of negative wishes: 'may there be no trick, and may there be no army.'
segments.s402-g81: همه دشت is a fronted noun phrase 'the whole plain' (همه modifying دشت). بی‌تن qualifies the following coordinated noun سر و یال — literally 'heads and manes without bodies.' The finite verb بود appears at the end in past tense, so the clause literally says 'the whole plain was heads-and-manes disembodied.'
segments.s402-g82: به جای دگر is an adverbial phrase 'in another place / elsewhere' (به + جای + دگر). گرز و گوپال is the coordinated noun phrase 'mace(s) and cowherd(s)' acting as the subject/complement, and بود is the past copula 'was' concluding the clause. Word order here is prepositional phrase → noun phrase → verb (poetic inversion).
segments.s402-g83: ز خون is an ablative/casual phrase 'from/because of the blood.' بر در دژ is a locative/against phrase 'upon the gate of the fortress' (بر = on/upon, در = gate). همی before the verb gives a continuous/progressive flavor and خاست is the past 'arose/rose' – the verb appears at the end as typical in classical Persian narrative.
segments.s402-g84: که introduces a result/relative clause 'so that/that.' دانست is a past transitive verb 'knew/recognized' and is placed before its subject in this poetic inversion; دست چپ (the left hand) is the grammatical subject and از دست راست ('from the right hand') expresses the standard of comparison or the thing distinguished. Overall: 'so that the left hand recognized (or could distinguish) the right hand.'
segments.s402-g85: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when.' اسفندیار is the subject. اندر آمد ز جای is a serial expression meaning 'entered / came forth from his place' (اندر = in/into, آمد = came, ز جای = from his place) — note the verb آمد in past at the clause end as normal.
segments.s402-g86: سپهدار کهرم is a noun phrase: سپهدار 'commander' modified by کهرم used adjectivally as 'aged/old' (literally 'commander of old age' = 'the aged commander'). بیفشارد is a past tense verb 'pressed/crushed' (here 'stamped'), and پای 'foot' is the object; Persian permits the object after the verb in poetry, so the clause reads 'the aged commander stamped (pressed) his foot.'
segments.s402-g87: دو جنگی is the plural subject 'two fighters/warriors' (جنگی = fighter). بران سان is an adverbial phrase meaning 'in that way / thus' (بران = 'upon/thereon/with that', سان = 'manner/like'), and برآویختند is the past plural verb 'they sprang upon / leapt at' placed at the end as expected in narrative poetry.
segments.s402-g88: که again introduces a comment/result clause 'so that / and,' گفتی here functions as a noun-like reference to 'what is said / as is told' (a conventional gloss meaning 'as one says' or 'as the story goes'), بهمشان 'to one another / among them' marks reciprocal relation, and برآمیختند is past plural 'they mingled/merged/entangled (with one another).' Together this yields a parenthetical 'and, as is said, they mixed together.'
segments.s402-g41: چو introduces a temporal clause = 'when'. کهرم is a proper name (subject) placed before the verb. بر expresses motion toward/to and links to باره دژ as the place reached. رسید is the past tense verb 'arrived/reached' and comes at the end as typical Persian word order.
segments.s402-g42: پس = 'then' (temporal connective). لشکر ایرانیان را is the definite object phrase ('the Iranian army' marked by را); the subject (Kahrum) is omitted and understood from the previous clause. بدید is past 'saw' (3rd person).
segments.s402-g43: چنین گفت = 'said thus' where the adverb چنین precedes the verb گفت. کاکنون = 'now/at this moment' (emphatic). به جز introduces an exception meaning 'except' and رزم کار = 'the business/affair of battle' (رزم qualifies کار).
segments.s402-g44: چه ماندست is an archaic/perfect-style expression meaning 'what remains/what is left' (ماندست = 'has remained/is left'). با introduces accompaniment 'with' and گرد اسفندیار means 'Esfandiyar's band/host' (گرد as 'band/host' is a poetic military term; here it marks Esfandiyar's retinue).
segments.s402-g45: همه تیغها = 'all the swords' (object placed before the verb for emphasis). برکشیم is a hortatory/1st-person-plural form 'let us draw' (برکش- + -یم). از نیام gives the source 'from the scabbard'.
segments.s402-g46: This clause is inverted for metre: literally 'to the dagger a message must be sent.' باید = 'it is necessary' and takes the bare past/infinitival verbal form فرستاد in older/poetic Persian to mean 'one must send.' به خنجر here indicates instrument/means ('with/by the dagger') and پیام = 'message/tidings.'
segments.s402-g47: چو = 'when' introducing the temporal clause. بخت is the agent 'fortune' and تاب = 'radiance/brightness' (object) which بخت 'brought' (آورد) into/on the face (به چهره ... اندر آورد = 'brought into the face' = 'shed light on his countenance'). The word order places the effect (تاب) and location (به چهره) before the verb for poetic effect.
segments.s402-g48: بران = 'upon that / thereon' linking to the previous event. نامداران = 'the renowned/noble men' (subject). ببد is the archaic past form of 'to be' = 'befell/wasfalling' here meaning 'a ... befell them.' کار سخت = 'a hard task' (predicate).
segments.s402-g73: سپه را (the host) is marked with the object/topic marker را and placed before the verb in a poetic, somewhat passive construction; به مرگ آمد literally 'came to death' = 'faced death' or 'was brought to death'. اکنون is an adverb 'now' and نیاز here is a noun meaning a cry/lament or supplication; in classical poetic word order the verb آمد can appear before the noun it relates to, so the clause reads roughly 'the host came to death; now a lament came.'
segments.s402-g74: ز + noun (ز خلخ) is a poetic preposition meaning 'from/of the anklet'. پر از is a fixed phrase 'full of', so پر از درد شد means 'became full of pain' with شد as the past-tense 'became'. تا here means 'up to'/'as far as' and طراز denotes the trimming/pattern/ornamentation; altogether the line says the anklets (or their sound) were filled with pain/sorrow reaching to the trimmings.
segments.s402-g75: ازان پس is a set phrase 'after that/thereupon'; همه is the subject 'all/everyone'; پیش‌مرگ literally 'those who go before/fore-runners' functions as 'vanguard' or 'front-line men'; آمدند is the plural past verb 'came'. Word order is typical classical Persian: temporal phrase + subject + predicate.
segments.s402-g76: زره‌دار is the noun 'armored (men)' or 'armored warrior(s)'; با introduces accompaniment 'with' and governs the list گرز و ترگ ('maces and rags/tattered things' — here likely martial accoutrements or torn banners); آمدند is again the plural past 'they came', agreeing with the (understood or preceding) plural subject. ترگ is an older/poetic word and appears as a companion item to گرز.
segments.s402-g77: ده و دار ('village and house') are two coordinated nouns meaning 'villages and dwellings/houses'; برخاست is the past singular 'rose/rose up' — in poetry a coordinated subject can take a singular verb or the verb may be used for stylistic effect; از رزمگاه gives the source 'from the battlefield'.
segments.s402-g78: هوا (the air/atmosphere/mood) is the subject and شد is the past 'became'; به کردار is a prepositional phrase 'in the manner/likeness of' (کردار used poetically as 'form/likeness'), and ابر سیاه 'a black cloud' is the simile. The clause means the atmosphere turned dark/gloomy like a black cloud.
segments.s402-g79: به هر جای is a locative phrase 'in/at every place'; بر توده 'on the heap/mass' (here the heaps of bodies) uses بر 'on/upon'; کشته بود is the past stative/compound 'were slain/lay dead' with بود marking past existence/state. The clause reports bodies everywhere on the piles.
segments.s402-g80: کسی را uses را after کسی for emphasis/definiteness in a classical register; کجا here is rhetorical ('anywhere/at all') rather than a simple location; روز 'that day' is temporal, and برگشته بود is the pluperfect 'had returned'. Together the inversion and particles give the meaning 'no one had returned that day' (a rhetorical negative statement).
segments.s402-g33: به لشکر is a prepositional phrase 'to the army' placed before the verb; چنین گفت is an inversion meaning 'said thus' (چنین = 'thus', گفت = past 'said'). کز is an archaic contraction (که + از) introducing the following clause and functions here as 'that/from' (i.e. 'that because of' or 'from'); خواب شاه is a genitive noun phrase 'the king's sleep/dream'.
segments.s402-g34: دل من پر از رنج شد is a full clause: دل من (my heart) + پر از رنج (full of sorrow) + شد (past of شدن = 'became'), so 'my heart became full of sorrow.' جان تباه is a verbless, elliptical clause where جان (life/soul) is qualified by the adjective تباه (ruined), i.e. 'my life is ruined' — this kind of nominal juxtaposition is common in classical Persian.
segments.s402-g35: کنون بی‌گمان sets the time and certainty ('now, undoubtedly'). باز here means 'back/again' (i.e. returning). باید + infinitive/nominal verb (here شدن) expresses necessity — an impersonal/obligatory sense: 'it is necessary to (be/return)' or idiomatically 'we must return.'
segments.s402-g36: ندانم is the present-negative first-person ('I do not know'). کزین پس is a fused classical expression (که از این پس) meaning 'after this' or 'henceforth'. چه شاید بدن is a subordinate interrogative/optative: چه = 'what', شاید = modal 'may/possibly' and بدن = 'the body' (i.e. 'what may become of the body'); the verb is elliptical/understood ('will happen to').
segments.s402-g37: بزرگان is the plural subject 'the great ones/nobles.' چنین is an adverb 'thus/so.' روی برگاشتند is an idiomatic sequence: روی ('face') + برگاشتند (past of برگشتن, 'they turned/returned'), literally 'they turned their faces' = 'they turned away/retreated'.
segments.s402-g38: به شب is a time phrase 'at night.' دشت پیکار is a noun phrase 'the plain of battle' (دشت + پیکار). بگذاشتند is past plural of گذاشتن with the prefix بـ (classical/perfective flavor) meaning 'they set/began' or 'they placed' — here 'they set (the) battle' i.e. 'they engaged/began fighting on the plain.'
segments.s402-g39: پس 'then' marks sequence. اندر is an archaic/prepositional particle 'in/into' (classical register). همی is the classical progressive/continuous particle; combined with آمد (past 'came'), همی آمد gives the simple past sense 'came in/entered' with a narrative flavor. اسفندیار is the subject (proper name).
segments.s402-g40: زره‌دار is a descriptive nominal 'armored one' (an epithet attached to the subject). با is the preposition 'with' indicating accompaniment or instrument. گرزه گاوسار is a noun + name: گرزه 'mace' modified by گاوسار, here treated as the name of the mace (proper name) — 'with the mace Gausar.'
segments.s402-g9: چو is the subordinate temporal marker 'when' placed before the verb. بشنید is a past third-person form 'he heard'. با اندریمان is the recipient/partner phrase 'to/with Andariman' (با marking the person addressed). بگفت is a past reporting verb 'he said'; together the clause reads 'When he heard, he said to Andariman...'.
segments.s402-g10: که introduces the content clause ('that...'). تیره شب is an adjective + noun 'dark night' (adjective precedes noun). آواز نتوان نهفت is a negative capability construction: نتوان (cannot) + نهفت (root/past-stem used here in the sense of 'be hidden/hidden') — literally 'a voice cannot be hidden.'
segments.s402-g11: چه گویی is an interrogative formula 'what do you say' (چه = 'what', گویی = archaic 2nd-person/present sense 'you say'). که introduces the subordinate clause that follows. امشب 'tonight' sets the time; چه شاید expresses uncertainty 'what/might perhaps' (چه here emphasizes 'what' in the sense of 'what is it that...'); بدن 'the body' is the noun of the clause, here meaning 'the (human) body/person' or 'those present'.
segments.s402-g12: بباید is a modal 'must/ought' (impersonal obligation). همی is an archaic emphatic particle ('indeed/just') modifying the following phrase. داستانها is the plural 'the tales/events/affairs'. زدن here takes the verbal sense 'to do/perform/tell' in the idiom 'داستان زدن' (i.e. to carry out or speak of such affairs); the construction 'بباید ... زدن' yields 'must (be) do(ne)/must perform or must tell these things.'
segments.s402-g13: که again introduces the clause. یارد is 'the companion/helper'. گشادن is the verbal stem 'to open' (here functioning as the action: 'opened'). بدین سان is the set phrase 'in this way' built from the prepositional prefix ب + دین (old/contracted form related to 'این') + سان ('manner'). دو لب = 'two lips' (i.e. both lips), the direct object of 'open' (i.e. 'opened his two lips').
segments.s402-g14: به + بالین is a locative phrase 'at the pillow/bedside' (به marking location). شاهی is adjectival 'royal' specifying the pillow: 'the king's pillow' (i.e. at the king's bedside). درین is the contracted preposition + demonstrative ('در این' = 'in this'). تیره‌شب is a compound 'dark-night' meaning 'this dark night'. The whole phrase gives the setting 'at the king's bedside in this dark night.'
segments.s402-g15: بباید فرستاد is an impersonal obligation 'one must send' or 'it is necessary to send.' تا introduces a purpose/result clause here ('so that/in order that'). هرک هست = 'whoever is there' (هرک = 'whoever/whichever', هست = 'is/exists').
segments.s402-g16: سرانشان is the object 'their heads' (سران = heads/leaders; here bodily heads). به خنجر marks the instrument 'with a dagger' (به used instrumentally). ببرند is a 3rd-person plural subjunctive/optative form 'may they take/carry/cut off' (here functioning as the action desired in the purpose clause introduced by تا). پست follows as an adverb/adjective 'low' / 'down' (i.e. to lay them low or treat them meanly).
segments.s402-g25: This whole phrase gives the cause/setting. ز بس (a poetic collocation) = 'because of/from so much' and takes the following noun نعره 'shouting' as its object; از هر سوی is a prepositional phrase 'from every side/direction' that further localizes the shouting; زین نشان is a poetic demonstrative phrase 'at that sign/sight' (زین = 'this/that' + نشان = 'sign/indication'). Word order places the causal preposition + noun before the directional phrase and the demonstrative.
segments.s402-g26: پر آواز شد is a predicative phrase: پر (here 'full of') modifies the noun آواز ('sound/voice'), and شد is the past tense of شدن 'became' (resultative 'was filled with sound'); گوش گردنکشان is a genitival/possessive phrase 'the ears of the گردنکشان' (those who raise their necks = 'the proud'), with the possessed noun preceding the possessor phrase, a common Persian order.
segments.s402-g27: سپه گفت is the main clause 'the army said'; ک is the cliticized form of که introducing reported speech ('that'). آواز بسیار گشت is the reported clause: آواز 'the sound' + بسیار گشت 'became great/excessive' where گشت is past of گشتن 'became/turned'.
segments.s402-g28: از اندازه پاسبان برگذشت is a short result/complement: از + اندازه 'beyond measure/extent' modifies the following noun phrase; پاسبان is the noun 'the guard', and برگذشت (past of برگذشتن) means 'passed beyond' or 'exceeded' — together: 'it exceeded the guard's measure/limit.'
segments.s402-g29: This is a hortative clause led by the temporal adverb کنون 'now'. دشمن 'the enemy' is the object, از خانه بیرون is a prepositional phrase 'out of the house', and کنیم is the 1st-person plural subjunctive/hortative of کردن 'let us do / let us cause' — together 'now let us drive the enemy out of the house.'
segments.s402-g30: ازان پس is a temporal connector 'after that / thenceforth'. برین چاره is a demonstrative + noun ('برین' = 'this' or 'upon this' + چاره 'remedy/expedient'), and افسون کنیم uses افسون (literally 'charm') with کنیم in the hortative/subjunctive 'let us enact/use' — here idiomatically 'let us try/use this stratagem.'
segments.s402-g31: دل کهرم is a genitival phrase 'the heart of کهرم' (a proper name or epithet); از پاسبان 'because of/from the guard' is an ablative complement giving cause; تنگ شد is 'became tight/constricted' — a past-state verb phrase indicating emotional constriction or distress.
segments.s402-g32: بپیچید is the past of پیچیدن here used intransitively 'he turned/turned away'; و is the coordinating 'and'; رویش is 'his face' (روی + enclitic pronoun ش); پر + آژنگ شد is a result-state: پر 'full (of)' + آژنگ (an archaic/poetic noun) + شد 'became' — 'his face became full of آژنگ' (i.e. filled with a strange color/appearance).

sentences.s402-s1: When the moon sank from the silver throne, three watches of the dark night passed.
sentences.s402-s2: Then the guard burst out loudly, crying that Gushtasp is king and fate is victorious.
sentences.s402-s3: When the Turks heard that cry, they all at once turned their ears to the sound.
sentences.s402-s4: Kahram's heart was stunned by the guard; his spirit was darkened by his voice.

sentences.s402-s51: No one knows the world's wish; it will not reveal the hidden to us.
sentences.s402-s52: Whoever slays a man deserves an ill outcome; the fleeing one keeps driving off at once.

sentences.s402-s36: When Arjasp was arrayed/prepared, (then) the heart/core — may there be no deceit and may there be no army.
